1: Standard revelation formula opening the parable of the two eagles. Chapter 17 presents Ezekiel's most elaborate mashal (riddle-parable): it requires two sections — the parable (Ezekiel 17:1-10) and its interpretation (Ezekiel 17:11-21) — followed by a messianic supplement (Ezekiel 17:22-24) that transforms the parable's meaning from judgment to eschatological hope.
2: chud chidah umshol mashal: 'Pose a riddle and speak a parable.' Chidah (riddle, enigma) and mashal (parable, proverb, figurative speech) are paired — the oracle is deliberately opaque, requiring interpretation. This double generic label prepares the audience for indirect communication: the message about Babylon, Zedekiah, and Egypt will first be veiled in animal imagery and only later decoded. The rhetorical distancing may allow the audience to evaluate the situation objectively before recognizing their own culpability.
3: hannesher hagadol godol hakanapaim erech ha'ever: 'The great eagle with great wings, long of wing.' Nesher (eagle or vulture) is the Hebrew term for the largest birds of prey; Nebuchadnezzar is compared to an eagle throughout the prophets (Jeremiah 48:40; Ezekiel 49:22; Habakkuk 1:8). Mele ha'notzah (full of long feathers) indicates the eagle's majesty and power — an imperial figure. Lebanon represents Jerusalem and Judah (cedar = royal/Davidic dynasty); the eagle coming to Lebanon is the Babylonian invasion of Judah (605-597 BCE).
4: et-tzameret haaraz qataf: 'He cropped off the top of the cedar.' Tzameret (topmost branch, top, crown) of the cedar = Jehoiachin, the young king taken into Babylonian exile in 597 BCE (2 Kings 24:12). The first eagle takes the top branch to the eretz kenaan (land of Canaan/merchants) and the ir rokhlin (city of merchants) = Babylon. The commercial language (kenaan as merchant, rochlim as traders) captures Babylon's status as the economic center of the ancient world.
5: vayikach mizera haarets: 'He also took from the seed of the land.' The second planting: after taking Jehoiachin, Nebuchadnezzar planted a new king from the royal line — Zedekiah (Mattaniah), Jehoiachin's uncle (2 Kings 24:17). Bezera haarets (from the seed of the land) refers to planting a Judean vassal rather than a Babylonian governor. Al-mayim rabbim (beside many waters) = the Babylonian arrangements for Judah — political stability under Babylonian oversight.
6: vatitzomach vatehiy legefen seruchah shiphelet qomah: 'It grew and became a spreading vine of low stature.' The vine (gefen) images Zedekiah's vassalage: he prospered but remained deliberately low (shiphelet qomah = low of height), spreading but not rising up. Litphenot darnanim alav (its branches turned toward the eagle) shows the vine's dependence on the Babylonian eagle. The shorashav tachtav (roots under him) suggests Zedekiah's entire stability was grounded in the Babylonian treaty.
7: vehinei nesher acher echad gadol: 'But behold, there was another eagle, great.' The second eagle is Pharaoh Hophra/Apries of Egypt (cf. Jeremiah 37:5-8; Ezekiel 44:30). The vine (Zedekiah) bent its roots toward this second eagle — seeking military alliance with Egypt against Babylon. Hishlacha shorasheiha alav (it stretched out its roots toward him) is the act of political treachery: breaking the sworn treaty with Babylon in favor of Egypt. The passive construction emphasizes the vine's own initiative — Zedekiah was not forced but chose the treacherous reorientation.
8: el-sadeh tovah al-mayim rabbim hi shetuah: 'It was planted in good soil beside many waters.' The description of Zedekiah's original planting (by Nebuchadnezzar, in Babylonian-protected Judah) as genuinely good and well-watered emphasizes that the problem was not the circumstance but the choice. Zedekiah had adequate resources and stability under Babylonian suzerainty; his reaching toward Egypt was gratuitous treachery, not desperate survival instinct.
9: emor hatzliach hatichlach: 'Say: Will it thrive? Will it not pull up its roots?' The rhetorical questions are ironic — any agricultural expert would know that a vine transplanted from good soil to a second location will not thrive. The withering of the vine despite the second eagle's protection is inevitable: Egypt will prove unable to protect Judah from Babylon (as Jeremiah 37:7-10 confirms). The political analysis is also theological: alliances that involve oath-breaking with Babylon are also oath-breaking before YHWH (Ezekiel 17:19).
10: vehineh shetuah hatzlach: 'And behold — it is planted. Will it thrive?' The vine will wither even in its new location (al-arugot tzimchah tiyaves). The east wind (ruach hakadim = the sirocco, hot dry wind from the desert = Babylon) will complete the destruction. The entirely agricultural metaphor maintains the parabolic distance — the audience evaluates the botany before they realize they are evaluating their own king's political theology.
11: vayehi devar YHWH elai lemor: 'And the word of YHWH came to me, saying.' The formula marks the transition from parable to interpretation, which is characteristic of Ezekiel's extended parables (cf. Ezekiel 24:15-27). The interpretation section decodes the botanical imagery systematically — each image identified with its historical referent — making Ezekiel's hermeneutics transparent and modeling a method of reading figurative prophecy.
12: emor na leveit hameri: 'Say now to the rebellious house.' Beit hameri (house of rebellion) is Ezekiel's constant characterization of Israel/Jerusalem (Ezekiel 2:3, 5, 6, 7, 8; Ezekiel 3:9, 26, 27). The interpretation is addressed directly to the exilic community already in Babylon — many of whom may have had hopes that Zedekiah's alliance with Egypt would produce a military reversal. The historical identification: the first eagle = Babylon's king; Lebanon's topmost branch = Jerusalem's king (Jehoiachin).
13: vayikach mizera hamemlucha: 'And he took from the royal seed.' The royal seed = Zedekiah (from the Davidic line). Vayikrot ito berit (he made a covenant with him) = the vassal treaty Nebuchadnezzar imposed on Zedekiah. The treaty is a formal berit (covenant, oath-bound agreement); its violation will carry the full weight of covenant-breaking. Vayavet oto be'alah (he brought him under oath) — the treaty was sworn before YHWH (the gods/YHWH being invoked as treaty witnesses).
14: liheyot mamlacha shephela: 'That it might be a lowly kingdom.' YHWH's actual design for Zedekiah was that of a lowly but stable vassal (shephela), not rising up but living quietly within the Babylonian framework. Lishmor et-berito (to keep his covenant) — the vassal covenant with Babylon is implicitly a covenant with YHWH's design for this season of judgment. Zedekiah's treaty-breaking with Babylon was therefore simultaneously a violation of the divinely-designed political arrangement.
15: vayimrod bo lishloach mal'achav Mitzraimah: 'But he rebelled against him by sending his messengers to Egypt.' The political treachery is described with the root marad (rebel) — the same word used for rebellion against YHWH. Sending to Egypt for horses (susim veam rav) was explicitly prohibited under the Deuteronomic law (Deuteronomy 17:16: “he shall not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt”). Zedekiah's Egyptian alliance violated both the Babylonian treaty and the Torah's prohibition of military dependence on Egypt.
16: chai ani ne'um adonai YHWH im-lo bemlachim hamemlich oto: 'As I live, declares the Lord YHWH: In the place of the king who made him king, in Babylon, he will die.' The divine oath formula seals the judicial sentence. The punishment fits the crime: Zedekiah who betrayed the king who made him king will die in Babylon. The irony is complete — his alliance with Egypt to escape Babylon results in his capture and death in Babylon (2 Kings 25:6-7).
17: velo bechayil gadol uvkahal rav yaaseh oto Pharo: 'Neither with great army nor great company will Pharaoh make [anything] for him.' Egypt's military assistance will be useless (lo yaaseh oto) — the promised horses and troops will not materialize effectively against Babylon (Jeremiah 37:7 records that Egypt briefly appeared and withdrew). The vine's fatal mistake was assuming Egypt could deliver what only YHWH can provide: security against the Babylonian instrument of divine judgment.
18: uvazah alah lahaper berit: 'He despised the oath by breaking the covenant.' Bazah alah (despised the oath/curse) — the alah (oath, curse) was the treaty-oath sworn before YHWH. To break it is not merely political perfidy but religious violation: treaty-oaths in the ancient Near East were sworn before the gods, and in Israel's case, before YHWH himself. Ezekiel's logic is that you cannot neutrally violate a Babylonian treaty if that treaty was sealed with YHWH as witness — to break the treaty is to despise YHWH's role as treaty-guarantor.
19: lachan koh amar adonai YHWH chai ani im-lo alati asher bazah ve'beriti asher hefer: 'Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: As I live, I will bring down upon his head the oath he despised and the covenant he broke.' This verse is theologically crucial: YHWH claims the violated treaty as his own (alati, my oath; beriti, my covenant). The Babylonian treaty became sacred through the swearing of an oath in YHWH's name. When Zedekiah broke it, he was not just politically treacherous but personally dishonoring YHWH, whose name was invoked in the covenant.
20: uferashti alav reshti venilkad bimtzudati: 'I will spread my net over him, and he will be caught in my snare.' YHWH's net is the characteristic image for inescapable divine judgment (cf. Ezekiel 12:13; Hosea 7:12; Lamentations 1:13). The capture of Zedekiah is recounted in 2 Kings 25:5-6 — he fled and was caught by Babylonian troops at Jericho. Ezekiel presents this military capture as YHWH's own action: the Babylonian army is the instrument; YHWH is the hunter. Vehaviotiv Bavel (I will bring him to Babylon) — judgment executed through historical agency is still YHWH's personal action.
21: umivchar migrachav bekhol-aguddav baherev yipolu: 'And all his best troops in all his squadrons will fall by the sword.' The military rout of Judah's army is included in the judgment sentence. Uvisharid yizru lekhol-ruach (the survivors will be scattered to every wind) — the scattering to the four winds is Ezekiel's characteristic image for exile (cf. Ezekiel 5:12; Ezekiel 12:14). The phrase veyadata ki ani YHWH dibarti (you will know that I, YHWH, have spoken) concludes the judgment section with the recognition formula.
22: koh amar adonai YHWH velakachti ani mitzameret haaraz haramah venatati: 'Thus says the Lord YHWH: I myself will take from the top of the cedar — from its high crown — and I will set it out.' The messianic supplement is introduced by the emphatic ani (I myself) — YHWH contrasts himself with the Babylonian eagle who took the previous top-branch (Jehoiachin). YHWH's own taking is a planting, not a deportation. Mi'tzameret roshey yeniqotav rach (from the topmost of its highest branches, a tender twig) = the messianic shoot from the Davidic line (cf. Isaiah 11:1; Jeremiah 23:5; Zechariah 3:8). The tenderness and smallness of the twig emphasizes that this planting will be YHWH's miracle, not political prowess.
23: behar merom Yisrael eshtelenhu: 'On the high mountain of Israel I will plant it.' Behar merom Yisrael (on the high mountain of Israel) = Mount Zion/the eschatological mountain of YHWH (Isaiah 2:2-3; Psalms 48:1-2). The planting on this mountain is the reversal of the Babylonian deportation to Babylon's “mountain of merchants.” The planted twig will become a noble cedar (erez addir) under which all birds will dwell — the language of the great world-tree (cf. Daniel 4:10-12; Matthew 13:32) signifying universal sovereignty. Every bird of every kind (kol tzipor kol-kanaf) will dwell in its shade — the messianic kingdom's universal reach.
24: veyadu kol-atsei hasadeh ki ani YHWH hishpalti etz gavoah: 'And all the trees of the field shall know that I, YHWH, have brought the high tree low and made the high tree low.' The recognition formula applied to the whole created order. The four reversals — high tree brought low, low tree raised high; green tree dried up, dry tree made to flourish — are the covenant's characteristic pattern (Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 2:4-8; the Magnificat in Luke 1:51-53). YHWH is the sovereign who reverses human assessments of power and permanence. The final seal: ani YHWH dibarti ve'asiti (I YHWH have spoken and I will do it) — the absolute certainty of divine speech becoming act.
